schema_notes: Per-billionaire record. Every dollar has source_url + retrieved_at. Events canonically scoped by event_role. Classification for Tier A/B/C happens downstream. NOTE: Leonard Lauder died 2025-06-14. This is a posthumous record; net-worth denominator is frozen at time of death. Forbes profile for a deceased individual typically delists; Bloomberg shows final figure.
generated_by: scrooge v3 manual deep research — single-subject + regen_v3@sha256-fc434da0c68e6ccd + regen_v3@sha256-bc1a13d5cfef6fb3

person.wealth_source: The Estée Lauder Companies — eldest son of Estée and Joseph H. Lauder; president 1972-1995, CEO 1982-1999, Chairman Emeritus thereafter. Primary asset: Estée Lauder Cos. (EL) stock via family holdings; Lauder family controls multi-class voting shares.
person.posthumous_note: Record generated 10 months after death; treat as frozen-denominator lifetime-giving assessment, not a live Forbes list entry. Forbes real-time billionaires removes deceased individuals.

net_worth.range_note: Forbes estimated $9.7B at death (June 2025). Bloomberg Billionaires Index showed $15.6B. ~60% spread is material and tracks the methodology gap between Forbes (applies larger illiquidity/control discounts to Estée Lauder stock) and Bloomberg (smaller discount). Jonah's ~$4-7B brief underestimates — actual range is ~$9.7-15.6B because EL stock recovered somewhat by June 2025 from 2024 lows. No post-death update; denominator is frozen.

sources.publisher: Bloomberg Billionaires Index (cited in ELC press release and obituaries)

net_worth.liquidity_notes: Concentrated in Estée Lauder Cos (public, NYSE:EL), but the family holds Class B super-voting shares — selling meaningfully would cede control. Assume 30% liquidity for 'giving capacity' purposes; remainder is control-constrained. Posthumously, estate disposition matters more than liquidity — see trusts_disclosed_existence_structure_unknown.

cited_events.recipient: Various (Aspen Institute, NYC cultural institutions, education, Jewish causes, disease research per Inside Philanthropy profile)
cited_events.note: Assets fell from $10.4M (2019) to $1.19M (2024). Foundation being wound down; Leonard's death 06/14/2025 noted in 2024 filing officer list.
Last year with extracted data. ProPublica flags organization as 'not listed in IRS most recent list' — likely wound down or reorganized after Evelyn Lauder's 2011 death.

cited_events.recipient: Metropolitan Museum of Art — Leonard A. Lauder Research Center for Modern Art
cited_events.amount_usd_note: 'More than $1 billion' per Met press release and multiple 2013 news reports. Collection: 78 works at 2013 announcement (Picasso 33, Braque 17, Gris 14, Leger 14); later references cite 81 pieces. Value is appraisal-based, NOT a market transaction. CRITICAL METHODOLOGY FLAG: non-cash art gifts at appraised value are valued based on insurance/appraisal figures and are subject to large uncertainty — comparable Picasso Cubist works have traded in wide ranges ($40M-$180M at auction). A rigorous rebuild of the basket at mean-of-comparable-sales prices could plausibly value the collection in $600M-$2B+ range. IRS allows charitable deduction at FMV for long-held appreciated art; this was a major tax-benefit transaction in addition to a philanthropic one.
cited_events.structure_note: Announced 2013-04-09 as a promised gift; Lauder retained some works in life (a common structure for major art gifts). Collection shown at Met in 2014 exhibition 'Cubism: The Leonard A. Lauder Collection.' Treat as DIRECT_GIFT with completion over 2013-2025 (works transferred progressively; bequest may have completed remaining transfers at death).

cited_events.donor_entity: American Contemporary Art Foundation, Inc. (Leonard Lauder, President)
cited_events.recipient: Whitney Museum of American Art — endowment
cited_events.note: Whitney's largest-ever gift at the time; Gansevoort Street building later named in Lauder's honor. Given via American Contemporary Art Foundation, a separate 501(c)(3) vehicle of Lauder's that we have NOT yet pulled 990 data for (EIN unknown — not located in ProPublica search; may be classified differently).

cited_events.donor_entity: Leonard A. Lauder (personal collection via ACAF)
cited_events.amount_usd_note: Not dollar-quantified in our sources. 125-work donation including Jasper Johns, Agnes Martin, Rothko, Warhol, Stella, Ruscha. Across Lauder's tenure, 760 works gifted personally plus ~188 via acquisition committees.
cited_events.dedup_note: NOT added to observable_giving rollup (unpriced). Noted for context. Listed ballpark in posthumous coverage as part of total art giving; if ever priced at even $200M (conservative for 125 major 20th-c. works), would increase observable by that amount.

cited_events.donor_entity: Leonard A. Lauder (personal or via foundation — not specified in announcement)
cited_events.recipient: University of Pennsylvania School of Nursing — Leonard A. Lauder Community Care Nurse Practitioner Program
cited_events.note: Largest-ever gift to an American nursing school. Structured for ~140 tuition-free fellows by 2027, two-year commitment to underserved communities. Structure (endowed professorship + ongoing operating support) suggests this was likely a multi-year pledge; disbursement schedule not in public announcement.

cited_events.donor_entity: Lauder Family (Leonard A. Lauder, Ronald S. Lauder, and third generation: William, Gary, Aerin, Jane)
cited_events.amount_attribution_method: Jonah-assumption: attribute ~3/8 to Leonard (eldest of two founding brothers plus four third-gen contributors). Announcement gives family total only; individual shares not disclosed. DEDUP RISK: some portion of Leonard's share may have already flowed through Lauder Foundation Inc 2023 grants ($1.5M) — likely not, given scale. Larger risk: the $200M is a COMMITMENT to be funded over time, not a lump disbursement.
cited_events.note: ADDF was co-founded 1998 by Leonard and Ronald Lauder in honor of their mother Estée's Alzheimer's. The $200M (April 2023) is described as 'committed' — funding schedule unclear. Lauder brothers + family have given smaller amounts over 25 years.

cited_events.donor_entity: Leonard A. Lauder (cumulative lifetime, all vehicles)
cited_events.amount_usd_note: 'Over $2 billion' cited in posthumous coverage (Chronicle of Philanthropy, CNN, Artnews obituaries). This is a journalist-assembled ballpark INCLUDING the Met $1.1B Cubist collection appraisal. Do NOT double-count against itemized events above.
cited_events.dedup_note: CONTEXT ONLY. Our itemized observable ($1.1B Met + $131M Whitney + $125M Penn + $75M ADDF share + ~$18M Lauder Foundation Inc rollup + $9.25M Leonard+Evelyn 2014-15 partial) = ~$1.46B. The obituary $2B is 37% higher, suggesting another ~$540M of gifts we have NOT itemized (likely pre-2019 Leonard+Evelyn Foundation giving 2001-2013 + sub-announcement gifts + smaller foundations not yet located).

cited_events.recipient: Various (990-PF aggregate; recipient detail not parsed)
cited_events.note: ProPublica 990-PF aggregate for fiscal-year 2015; end-year assets $41.6M; payout rate 85.0%.

pledges_and_announcements.status_source: Direct URL check: https://www.givingpledge.org/pledger/leonard-lauder returns 404. Same for https://www.givingpledge.org/pledger/ronald-lauder. No Lauder family member found in Giving Pledge signatory lists by Brave Search or Wikipedia pledger-list cross-check.
pledges_and_announcements.note: Verified not a Giving Pledge signatory. His brother Ronald Lauder also has not signed. This is POSITIVE for Leonard's record relative to the pledge-breach penalty: he did not make a public 50% promise and therefore cannot be scored as having broken one. He should NOT receive the PLEDGE_UNFULFILLED red flag.

political_giving.substitution_note: Not counted as philanthropy. NBER W26616 estimates political giving displaces charitable giving at ~$0.33 per $1. Leonard's political giving is small relative to his art/medical philanthropy.
political_giving.observed_total_note: Rough lifetime estimate. Campaign Money shows $279,900 in 2016 cycle alone. Across 2000-2024 cycles at similar cadence, ~$1-2M total. This is BELOW Ellison's $31M 5-year total by ~20x. Did not dominate his giving profile. FEC direct-pull not yet run.

foundations_active.name: Lauder Foundation Inc
foundations_active.established_year: ~1960s (founded by Estée and Joseph Lauder generation; Leonard succeeded as president)
foundations_active.officers_compensation: ZERO_COMP (Leonard Lauder as President/Director, plus Ehrenkranz, Schiele, Krulewitch, Somar, Dieterich — all zero)
foundations_active.payout_rate_notes: 2024 payout = $520K grants / $1.19M assets = 44% (well above 5% minimum); 2019 was $3.8M/$10.4M = 36%. Asset decline 2019→2024 of 89% reflects deliberate spend-down rather than underpayout.
foundations_active.red_flag_daf_transfers: NOT_VERIFIED (grant recipient list not parsed from 990-PF PDF; ProPublica summary does not enumerate recipients)

foundations_terminated_or_dormant.name: Leonard And Evelyn Lauder Foundation
foundations_terminated_or_dormant.status: apparently terminated or dormant post-2015 (ProPublica flags 'not listed in IRS most recent list')
foundations_terminated_or_dormant.note: Evelyn Lauder (Leonard's first wife) died 2011 — breast-cancer research advocate. Foundation likely wound up after her death; assets may have migrated to new vehicles. Pre-2015 grant totals: 2014 $2.5M, 2013 $1.3M, 2012 $6.3M, 2011 $6.3M. Total 2011-2015 = $23.2M observable.

foundations_not_yet_located_in_propublic.role: Vehicle through which Leonard made the $131M Whitney 2008 gift
foundations_not_yet_located_in_propublic.ein: UNKNOWN — not located in ProPublica or IRS TEO search with exact name. May be registered under alternate name or discontinued.
foundations_not_yet_located_in_propublic.todo: Search NY state charities registry and IRS BMF by EIN range. Possible candidate is the Lauder Foundation Inc itself repurposed.

detected_vehicles.dafs_note: No donor-advised fund activity identified. For an art-focused major donor, direct museum gifts and named-vehicle foundations are the canonical structure; DAFs less typical. Absence-of-evidence only.
detected_vehicles.llcs_note: No philanthropic LLC identified. Lauder family operated through traditional 501(c)(3) foundations.
detected_vehicles.offshore_note: ICIJ Offshore Leaks name-match NOT YET RUN. Absence of public reporting so far.

trusts_disclosed_existence_structure_unk.source_quote: Implied by Leonard's will/estate — no public disclosure of structure as of 2026-04. Given ~$9.7-15.6B estate, Estée Lauder Cos multi-class share structure, and family-control priorities, expect a combination of family/dynasty trusts, charitable bequests (art completion transfers to Met and Whitney), and foundation top-ups.
trusts_disclosed_existence_structure_unk.classification_note: CRITICAL uncertainty. The Met Cubist collection's ~$1.1B promised-gift completion likely happens via estate. Additional bequests to Whitney, Penn, ADDF are plausible but not publicly disclosed. 2026-04-24: estate disposition not yet reported in public press.

red_flags.evidence: Met Cubist collection gift (2013) is ~75% of Leonard's observable giving and valued at $1.1B on appraisal rather than market transaction. Comparable-basket rebuild could produce $600M-$2B range. The denominator for the Scrooge ratio is highly sensitive to this single non-cash item.

$200M ADDF pledge (2023) announced as 'Lauder family' — six named donors across two generations. Attribution of individual shares is assumption-driven (we used 3/8 = $75M for Leonard; actual could be 50%, could be 15%).

Lauder Foundation Inc holds $1.19M in 2024 assets on a ~$10B personal net worth — <0.012%. Direct gifts (Met, Whitney, Penn) are Leonard's primary channel, not foundation accumulation. This is a disclosure/transparency observation, not a Scrooge signal per se.

red_flags_not_present: GIVING_PLEDGE_UNFULFILLED (never signed the Pledge — no promise to break)
LOW_PAYOUT (Lauder Foundation Inc 2024 payout 44% of assets)
POLITICAL_HEAVY (~$1-2M lifetime political giving, dwarfed by philanthropy)

rollup.observable_giving_method: sum(Met Cubist 2013 $1.1B + Whitney 2008 $131M + Penn 2022 $125M + ADDF 2023 Leonard-share $75M + Lauder Foundation Inc grants_out 2019-2024 $13.28M + Leonard+Evelyn Lauder Foundation grants 2011-2015 $23.2M). Excludes: 2002 Whitney 125-work donation (unpriced); pre-2011 giving; any bequests completed after death; ADDF and Met completion transfers still pending at death.
observable_giving_todo: Locate and pull American Contemporary Art Foundation 990s (EIN unknown).
Pull Leonard+Evelyn Lauder Foundation 990-PFs 2001-2010 and 2016-2017 — likely missing ~$50-100M in mid-range grants.
Re-price the 2013 Met Cubist collection at low/high bounds ($600M low / $2B high) to build uncertainty interval.
Obtain individual attribution of the $200M ADDF pledge (3/8 assumption).
Check 2002 Whitney 125-work art donation for public appraisal figure.
Check posthumous estate filings once public (likely 2026-2027).
rollup.observable_range_method: Low = itemized with Met at $600M + ADDF Leonard share at $25M (15%). High = itemized with Met at $2B + ADDF Leonard share at $100M (50%) + $200M for inferred unlocated 2001-2010 LEVF grants. The 2.5x range illustrates that ART-HEAVY PHILANTHROPY has inherently wider confidence intervals than cash-foundation giving.
rollup.expected_giving_method: 10% × liquid_wealth × min(years_as_bil/10, 1) where liquid_wealth = 0.30 × $9.7B = $2.91B; tenure cap saturated (30 years as billionaire). Expected = $291M. But this materially understates for lifetime-giving assessment of a deceased donor: a lifetime norm would expect ~30-50% of current liquid wealth given over 30 years ($870M-$1.45B). Jonah's 'On Track' hypothesis maps to the lifetime norm, not the annual 10% norm.
rollup.observable_ratio_to_expected_note: 5x the strict annual norm. Even using the liberal lifetime norm of ~$1.45B, Leonard's observable $1.46B meets it. By the conservative/low-bound Met appraisal ($600M Met instead of $1.1B), he would be at $963M — still above 10% annual norm but below lifetime norm.
rollup.observable_ratio_to_current_nw_note: 15% of Bloomberg $15.6B / Forbes $9.7B final estimate. For comparison: Gates ~100%, Buffett ~40%, Ellison ~0.2%.

hidden_upper_usd.offshore_uplift_note: Not yet checked (Phase 2). Zero until checked.
hidden_upper_usd.anonymous_cohort_method: Older HNW art-world donors give substantial anonymous support. Cohort estimate 2% of lifetime observable = $29M. Widened to $200M upper bound to cover 2001-2010 LEVF grants we haven't pulled and additional unannounced museum and medical gifts visible in Maine-coast and NYC cultural coverage.
hidden_upper_usd.religious_note: Leonard was Jewish; obituaries and JTA reference Jewish philanthropy. No disclosed tithing pattern, but typical HNW Jewish giving is 1-3% of lifetime wealth. Conservative upper bound $50M on $15.6B.
hidden_upper_usd.art_gift_revaluation_note: If Met Cubist collection is worth $2B rather than $1.1B (plausible given 2024 auction prices for Picasso Cubism), additional $900M in observable. Captured here as upper-bound only because we cite the $1.1B 2013 appraisal in itemized events.
hidden_upper_usd.trust_outcome_method: Estate disposition pending. Plausible charitable bequests: Met residual Cubist transfers, Whitney continued support, ADDF funding schedule completion, Penn ongoing, Judy Glickman Lauder's retained interest. Conservative estate charitable bequest: $1.5B. Uncertain whether this lands in his name or her/heirs' control.
hidden_upper_usd.total_method: anonymous + religious + art-revaluation + trust-outcome (DAF/LLC/offshore all zero). Note: art-revaluation and trust-outcome partially overlap; see caveat.

rollup.tier_rule: Leonard meets the 10% annual norm on observable alone ($1.46B vs $291M expected = 5x). He meets the lifetime norm (~$1.45B observable ≈ $1.45B expected). The Met Cubist collection is methodologically the most important caveat: a sophisticated version of this methodology would discount appraisal-based non-cash art gifts by 20-40% relative to cash gifts because the donor retains tax benefits and the museum's realized value is non-fungible. Even with a 40% haircut on the $1.1B Met gift (=$440M recognized), total observable falls to ~$800M — still 2.75x expected. Posthumous status further secures the tier: Leonard gave AT LEAST 15% of his lifetime peak wealth observably before death, with additional bequests likely in progress.
rollup.tier_published_caveat: Leonard Lauder is the canonical example of 'art-heavy lifetime giving that appears ON-TRACK even though the headline number depends on a single appraisal-based non-cash gift.' Readers should see his $1.1B Met Cubist collection as an IMPORTANT but CONTINGENT piece of the observable total — a stricter valuation approach would materially shift the ratio.

scrooge_legacy_score_internal_only.note: Giving gap: observable 1.46B / expected 0.29B > 1 → gap = 0. Tenure: 30 years as billionaire, saturated penalty 20. No pledge breach (never signed). Red flags are methodology observations only, not behavior flags → 0. Total ≈ 18-20. Posthumous adjustment: tenure penalty is arguable for a deceased lifetime-giver who has already discharged their giving; consider discounting to 0, yielding a final ~0-2 score (very low = NOT Scrooge-like).

right_of_reply.note: Subject is deceased. Right-of-reply should go to The Estée Lauder Companies (Chairman Emeritus office) and/or to Leonard's surviving family (Judy Glickman Lauder, William Lauder, Gary Lauder) if publication requires living-representative comment. For posthumous profiles, standard practice is to flag as such and proceed.
right_of_reply.planned_outreach_route: ELC corporate communications + Judy Glickman Lauder Foundation (if response desired).

summary_150_words: Leonard Lauder (1933-2025), Estée Lauder heir and Chairman Emeritus, died June 14, 2025 at 92 with a net worth of $9.7B (Forbes) to $15.6B (Bloomberg). Unlike his brother Ronald, Leonard never signed the Giving Pledge — both /pledger URLs return 404. But unlike Ellison, he delivered: observable lifetime giving totals ~$1.46B itemized ($1.1B 2013 Met Cubist collection, $131M 2008 Whitney endowment, $125M 2022 Penn nurse practitioner program, $75M attributed share of $200M 2023 ADDF family gift, plus foundation disbursements). This meets both the 10% annual norm and lifetime giving norm on ~$15B peak wealth. His small Lauder Foundation Inc ($520K grants 2024, spending down) was the minor channel; direct museum and university gifts were the primary vehicle. Foundation officers all zero-comp. Major methodology caveat: 75% of observable is the 2013 non-cash Met gift at appraisal value — comparable-basket rebuild could range $600M-$2B. Tier: On Track.
